raw_case:
Left lateral-aortic paraganglioma in an adolescent: A rare case report


Introduction

Paraganglioma is a rare neuroendocrine tumor arising outside the adrenal gland from the primitive neural crest cells. The pediatric form is an exceptional entity.

Case report

Here we report a case of a 15-year-old boy presenting with hypertensive crisis. Computed tomography scan revealed a left lateral-aortic paraganglioma, with significantly elevated plasmatic catecholamine levels. Preoperative management was conducted in our patient with selective alpha-1 blockers, then complete resection of the tumor was achieved without complications. The histological and immunohistochemical examination confirmed the diagnosis of a paraganglioma.

Discussion

Paranganglioma management should be started as soon as possible to prevent high blood pressure complications. It is based on preoperative medication with alpha-blocker to prevent adrenegeric discharge, followed by a complete and safe surgical removal of the tumor.

Conclusion

Functional paraganglioma remains a rare entity among pediatric population. Preoperative management is mandatory to avoid postoperative morbidity.

Highlights

•A rare case of lateral-aortic paraganglioma in an adolescent which is rarely reported in the literature and which is the first Moroccan case to our knowledge.•Our experience in the management of functional paraganglioma in an adolescent boy.•The importance of pre operative management to prevent post operative morbidity.1

Introduction

Paragangliomas are a very rare entity of neuroendocrine catecholamine-secreting tumors, especially in children. The retroperitoneal space is a rare location in pediatric solid tumors.A clear understanding of each diagnosis step is essential to distinguish paragangliomas from other common pediatric neoplasms. The treatment is based on surgery conducted by an experienced surgical team after adequate preoperative medical preparation. Genetic testing should be considered in all children because of the high risk of an underlying genetic disease. We report our experience in the management of a latero-aortic paraganglioma diagnosis in a 15-year-old boy, who presented with a hypertensive crisis.This work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [1].2

Case report

We report the case of a 15-year-old adolescent boy with no previous medical history. One month before his admission, he reported abdominal pain with worsening headaches. He also suffered from a convulsive seizure revealing a hypertensive peak complicated by a status epilepticus. The patient was then admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) for hypertensive encephalopathy.An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan (CT) was performed. It revealed a left, roughly rounded, well-limited, retroperitoneal mass with regular contours. The mass had liquid density and thickened walls, with contrast enhancement, measuring 45*40*37 mm. This formation was intimately adherent to the left renal artery and vein, which remained permeable. It also abutted the corporal-caudal region of the pancreas without a cleavage plane. In the posterior region, the tumor exerted an important mass effect on the left kidney. In the anterior area, it displaced the gallbladder coves and abutted the inferior mesenteric vein (Fig. 1). The adrenal glands appeared to be normal. After the stabilization of his blood pressure (BP), he was transferred to our Endocrinology department for further investigation.Upon admission, the blood pressure was at 160/90 mmHg, the heart rate at 98 beats per minute, the respiratory rate at 22 breaths per min, and the temperature at 37,5 °C. On abdominal examination, there was left periumbilical sensitivity and left lumber region percussion pain. Moreover, the patient reported an occasional triad of Menard (headache, sweating and palpitations).Normetanephrines were elevated at 2.8 times the normal range, (i.e. 3.02 nmol/l;NV: <1.07 nmol/l, Liquid Chromatography with tandem Mass Spectrometry) and metanephrines were normal. Moreover, the Hemoglobin A1C was at 6 %, with a fasting blood glucose of 65 mg/l. The calcitonin assay was negative, and the genetic testing was performed.The patient was started on Doxazosin, at a dose of 1 mg daily with strict monitoring of the BP. This medication allowed progressive control of the BP and was continued for 15 days. Afterward, the patient underwent an open surgery. The incision was above and below the umbilicus. The tumor was first freed from the posterior surface of the pancreatic tail. The mass appeared to be involving the inferior polar artery, which was ligated first, followed by the middle capsular artery. The tumor removal was complete.Gross histopathological evaluation revealed a mass weighing 52 g and measuring 6x5x3 cm, well-defined, of a fleshy appearance when cut and of a greyish-yellow color (Fig. 2). The microscopic examination showed a tumor proliferation, entirely encapsulated by a fibrous capsule. It was made up of large polygonal, sometimes spindle-shaped cells with abundant finely granular cytoplasm and finely nucleated ovoid nuclei. The stroma was thin and vascular. The complementary immunohistochemical study showed the presence of anti-chromogranin, anti-synaptophysin, and anti-PS 100 antibodies. Anti-Ki67 and anti-pan cytokeratin antibodies were negative. These findings confirmed the diagnosis of paraganglioma.The alpha-blocker was discontinued postoperatively. The BP levels remained stable after discontinuation of all anti-hypertensive medication. Measurements of metanephrines and normetanephrine were performed on the 10th postoperative day.3

Discussion

Paraganglioma is a rare neuroendocrine tumor derived from the extra-adrenal sympathetic and parasympathetic paraganglia. It is a very rare entity, with an incidence of 0.2 to 0.5 cases per million [[2], [3], [4], [5]]. The average age at presentation in pediatric population is about eleven to thirteen years old [3]. Our patient was diagnosed at the age of fifteen.The retroperitoneal location is the most frequent in catecholamine-secreting paragangliomas in children, accounting for 75 % of extra adrenal tumors, as it was the case for our patient and other cases from Spanish and Chinese series [[6], [7], [8]]. In the literature, a Spanish case series reported an adolescent girl aged 14 years old who presented with a functional retroperitoneal mass in the left paraaortic region. Moreover, a chinese series reported 3 cases of paraganglioma, also with a retroperitoneal location.In the existing literature, there is no sex predominance. However, a male preponderance was suggested by the National Registry of Childhood Cancers during childhood, followed by a female preponderance in the productive years, which results in an equal sex incidence overall [5,9]. This data is inconsistent with our case, involving an adolescent boy.In pediatric patients, paragangliomas are often part of a genetic susceptibility syndrome as 50 % to 70 % of paragangliomas are driven by a germline mutation. The most frequent susceptibility genes in which mutations may be identified are VHL, SDHD, SDHB, SDHC, and SDHAF2 [10]. In our patient, the results of genetic testing are still ongoing.As in adults, young patients also may present with symptoms linked to the catecholamines hypersecretion. Sometimes symptoms are also linked to the tumor mass effect on adjacent organs which were described in about 30 % of children presenting with paraganglioma [4,11,12].Our patient also reported abdominal pain as the first symptom, then a hypertensive crisis occurred, which is the most dreaded complication. In fact, hypertension is the most common sign as paragangliomas account for 1,7 % of secondary hypertension etiologies in childhood [3]. Other symptoms may be associated with hypertension, including headache and sweating forming the “classic Menard triad”, which was reported by our patient. In the literature, the Menard triad in children was recorded in 54 % of pediatric patients [12].Confirming the hypersecretion of catecholamines is the first step to corroborate functional paraganglioma diagnosis. It includes the measurement of metanephrines and normetanephrines [[13], [14], [15]]. As the 24-hour urine collection is difficult in children, plasma assay is often the preferred method, as was the case in our patient, in whom the results were positive for normetanephrines. In addition to clinical history and biochemical testing, multimodal imaging is important to the successful diagnosis and management of paraganglioma. The CT scan is the gold standard imaging technique for retroperitoneal paragangliomas in children. The density is usually >10 HU, with important contrast enhancement due to a rich capillary network, and delayed washout [17]. The CT scan performed in our patient revealed a left, roughly rounded, well-limited, retroperitoneal mass with a liquid density, measuring 45*40*37 mm. The mass was adhering to the adjacent organs, including left renal vessels, and the corporal-caudal region of the pancreas.Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is indicated in case of contraindication to CT scan, or in synchronous forms of paraganglioma, particularly for cervical and skull base location, which were not found in our patient's case, as the skull base MRI examination was negative [14,17,18].Preoperative medical management aims to control BP for at least 2 weeks before surgery with Selective alpha 1-blockers [11,17,19]. The drug of choice in children is Doxazosin. The treatment can be gradually increased with monitoring of BP and heart rate. The usual dose is 1 to 2 mg per day [10,11,19]. This preoperative management was conducted in our patient. A Brazilian series of 17 children and adolescents with pheochromocytoma used selective alpha-1 blockers (Doxazosin or Prazosin) in all patients for 14 days and interrupted 12 h before surgery [20]. On another Spanish case series, Phenoxybenzamine was used as a preoperative therapy for 14 days, followed by a surgical excision [7].Surgery is the treatment of choice for paraganglioma, but it is a high-risk surgical procedure and requires an experienced surgical team [21]. Open surgery is usually recommended, but the laparoscopic approach is the most common one used. In fact, either the Brazilian or the Spanish series used the laparoscopic approach, whereas an open surgery was performed in our patient due to the large size of the tumor and imagiological suspicious of pancreas invasion (absence of cleavage plane in CT scan) [6,20].Histopathologically, paragangliomas are hypervascular with chief cells which are often positive on immunohistochemistry [22]. That was the case for our patient, whose immunohistochemical staining was positive for Chromogranin and Synatptophysin, and negative for pan-cytokeratin, which helps distinguish paragangliomas from other neuroendocrine tumors [22].Children and adolescents require lifelong follow up after surgery, regardless of the identification of germline mutations [23]. Assaying plasma or urinary levels of metanephrine and normetanephrine is recommended within 2–6 weeks of surgery. Recommendations suggest an interval of 1–2 years for imaging tests, to avoid radiation exposure risks [23]. In our patient, there were no signs of recurrence during 18 months of follow-up.4

Conclusion

Our case highlights the severity of functional paraganglioma in children whose prognosis can be fatal due to the severity of the high blood pressure. Its management is truly challenging and requires a multidisciplinary approach.

Parental consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient's parents/legal guardian for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

This is a case report that does not require a formal ethical committee approval. Data were anonymously registered in our database. Access to data was approved by the head of the department.

Guarantor

Pr Hanane Latrech.

Funding

This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Author contribution

Dr. Nada El Yamani is the first author, she wrote the manuscript.Dr. Salma Derbel helped in writing the manuscript.Dr. Imane Assarrar worked on the conceptualization and helped in writing the manuscript and the literature review.Prof Nissrine Bouichrat helped in the conceptualization and supervised the redaction and reviewed the manuscript.Prof Siham Rouf helped in the conceptualization and supervised the redaction and reviewed the manuscript.Prof Hanane Latrech helped in the conceptualization and methodology, supervised the redaction, and reviewed and approved the final draft of the manuscript.All authors approved the final version of the manuscript.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 15-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Hypertensive crisis.
- **History of Present Illness:** One-month history of abdominal pain, worsening headaches, and a convulsive seizure leading to hypertensive encephalopathy diagnosed via ICU admission.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Blood pressure 160/90 mmHg, heart rate 98 bpm, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, temperature 37.5°C. Left periumbilical sensitivity and left lumbar percussion pain present. Occasional symptoms of Menard triad (headache, sweating, palpitations) reported.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Elevated plasma normetanephrines (2.8 times the normal range; 3.02 nmol/L).
  - Abdominal CT scan: 45x40x37 mm retroperitoneal mass near left renal artery, vein, and pancreas, with liquid density and contrast enhancement.
  - Negative Metanephrines, calcitonin, and anti-pan cytokeratin staining.
  - Histopathology: Mass measured 6x5x3 cm, encapsulated, vascular tumor with chromogranin- and synaptophysin-positive cells. Ki67 and pan-cytokeratin markers were negative.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - The hypertensive crisis, elevated catecholamines, abdominal mass, and Menard triad prompted suspicion of a functional catecholamine-secreting tumor (pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma).
   - Other possible causes like renal artery stenosis, adrenal cortical tumors, or neuroblastoma were considered but deprioritized based on imaging and biochemical findings.

2. **Diagnostic Refinement:**
   - Urinary or plasma catecholamines confirmed hypersecretion typical of paraganglioma, reinforced by elevated normetanephrines.
   - The CT imaging identified a retroperitoneal mass without adrenal involvement, strongly favoring paraganglioma over pheochromocytoma.

3. **Exclusion of Other Causes:**
   - Neuroblastoma was ruled out based on immunohistochemistry and non-malignant pathology (absence of small blue round cells or pan-cytokeratin positivity). 
   - Renal artery stenosis and other renal causes were excluded given normal renal arterial patency and invasive pancreas findings on CT.
   - No adrenal abnormalities excluded adrenal cortical tumors.

4. **Preoperative Confirmation:**
   - Plasma normetanephrines, imaging findings, and symptoms supported functional paraganglioma diagnosis, confirmed after surgical resection and histopathological analysis.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. So the patient is a 15-year-old male with a hypertensive crisis. His main symptoms are abdominal pain, headaches, and a seizure leading to hypertensive encephalopathy. Hmm, hypertensive encephalopathy usually happens when there's a sudden severe increase in blood pressure, causing brain swelling. But why would a young guy with no past medical history have such a crisis?

Looking at the history, he had a month of abdominal pain and worsening headaches. Then a seizure. The Menard triad—headache, sweating, palpitations—those are classic for pheochromocytoma. But wait, the triad is typically headache, sweating, and tachycardia, right? So maybe that's a clue here. 

The labs showed elevated plasma normetanephrines. That's a breakdown product of norepinephrine. Pheochromocytoma and paragangliomas (together called PPGLs) are tumors that secrete catecholamines, leading to hypertension. But wait, the metanephrines were negative? Wait, the test says elevated normetanephrines but metanephrines negative. Metanephrine is from epinephrine, and normetanephrine from norepinephrine. So maybe this tumor is producing more norepinephrine than epinephrine.

The CT scan found a retroperitoneal mass near the left renal artery and vein and pancreas. The location is important. Pheos are usually in the adrenal medulla, but paragangliomas can be extra-adrenal, like in the abdomen along the sympathetic chain. This mass is near the left renal area, which is a common site for paragangliomas. But wait, adrenal vs. extra-adrenal. Let's check the histopathology.

The histo report says encapsulated vascular tumor with chromogranin and synaptophysin positive. Those are neuroendocrine markers. So chromogranin and synaptophysin positivity supports a neuroendocrine tumor, like pheo or paraganglioma. Ki67 negative suggests low proliferation, which is typical for benign tumors. Pan-cytokeratin negative—so not a carcinoma, which would often express cytokeratin. 

But wait, the mass is in the retroperitoneum, not the adrenal gland. So if it's a paraganglioma, that would be an extra-adrenal pheochromocytoma. But pheochromocytoma is specifically adrenal. Paragangliomas can be sympathetic (secreting catecholamines) or parasympathetic (non-secreting). Since this patient has hypertension and elevated normetanephrines, it's a functioning sympathetic paraganglioma.

Negative calcitonin rules out medullary thyroid cancer, which can also present with hypertension but would have elevated calcitonin. Anti-pan cytokeratin negative—again, not a carcinoma. So the combination of location, hormone levels, and markers points to a paraganglioma.

The diagnosis would be a paraganglioma. Since it's extra-adrenal and secreting norepinephrine (hence elevated normetanephrine), causing the hypertensive crisis and associated symptoms. The presence of the Menard-like symptoms supports this. The histopathology confirms neuroendocrine origin with appropriate markers. So the final diagnosis is paraganglioma.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s hypertensive crisis, Menard triad symptoms (headache, sweating, palpitations), and elevated plasma normetanephrines strongly suggest a catecholamine-secreting tumor, such as pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma.  
<step 2> The retroperitoneal mass near the left renal artery/vein and pancreas on CT, combined with absent adrenal involvement, localizes the tumor to an extra-adrenal site, favoring paraganglioma over adrenal pheochromocytoma.  
<step 3> Histopathology showing chromogranin- and synaptophysin-positive cells confirms neuroendocrine origin, while negative pan-cytokeratin excludes carcinomas. The absence of Ki67 proliferation marker aligns with a benign or low-grade tumor.  
<step 4> Elevated normetanephrines (norepinephrine metabolite) with negative metanephrines (epinephrine metabolite) indicate predominant norepinephrine secretion, characteristic of paragangliomas.  
<step 5> Exclusion of other causes (e.g., medullary thyroid carcinoma via negative calcitonin) and the absence of alternative diagnoses solidify the conclusion.  

### Answer:
Paraganglioma

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s hypertensive crisis, Menard triad symptoms (headache, sweating, palpitations), and elevated plasma normetanephrines strongly suggest a catecholamine-secreting tumor, such as pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma.

The retroperitoneal mass near the left renal artery/vein and pancreas on CT, combined with absent adrenal involvement, localizes the tumor to an extra-adrenal site, favoring paraganglioma over adrenal pheochromocytoma.

Histopathology showing chromogranin- and synaptophysin-positive cells confirms neuroendocrine origin, while negative pan-cytokeratin excludes carcinomas. The absence of Ki67 proliferation marker aligns with a benign or low-grade tumor.
Elevated normetanephrines (norepinephrine metabolite) with negative metanephrines (epinephrine metabolite) indicate predominant norepinephrine secretion, characteristic of paragangliomas.
Exclusion of other causes (e.g., medullary thyroid carcinoma via negative calcitonin) and the absence of alternative diagnoses solidify the conclusion.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Suspicion of a functional catecholamine-secreting tumor (pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma) due to hypertensive crisis, elevated catecholamines, abdominal mass, and Menard triad.
Step 2: Deprioritized other possible causes like renal artery stenosis, adrenal cortical tumors, or neuroblastoma based on imaging and biochemical findings.
Step 3: Confirmed hypersecretion of catecholamines through urinary or plasma catecholamines, with elevated normetanephrines supporting paraganglioma.
Step 4: Identified a retroperitoneal mass via CT imaging without adrenal involvement, favoring paraganglioma over pheochromocytoma.
Step 5: Ruled out neuroblastoma based on immunohistochemistry and non-malignant pathology.
Step 6: Excluded renal artery stenosis and other renal causes due to normal renal arterial patency and invasive pancreas findings on CT.
Step 7: No adrenal abnormalities excluded adrenal cortical tumors.
Step 8: Preoperative confirmation of functional paraganglioma diagnosis through plasma normetanephrines, imaging findings, and symptoms.
Step 9: Final diagnosis confirmed after surgical resection and histopathological analysis: Lateral-aortic functional paraganglioma.
